target.name: 9 Mothers
target.founder: Russell Smith, Roman Khomenko, Bogdan Pyzh

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $4M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Defense & Security. Range: $60M - $500M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use

private.name: Tenna Systems
private.rationale: Same sector (Defense & Security), same stage (Seed), post-money $60M

data.narrative:
9 Mothers is a Seed-stage Defense & Security company founded by Russell Smith, Roman Khomenko, and Bogdan Pyzh that has raised $4.5M to date. The valuation analysis draws from a comparable set spanning 10 private companies and 2 public entities (DRSHF, IDEAFORGE.NS) operating in the same sector, with peer staging concentrated at Series A and Seed.

Two triangulation methods were applied. The Stage-Progression method uses the $4.5M total raised as a valuation proxy and applies a 2.5x multiplier typical for Seed-to-next-stage advancement, yielding $11M. The Peer-Median method references four same-sector, same/adjacent-stage comparables (Stark Defence at $500M, Vatn Systems at $300M, Ares Industries at $150M, and Tenna Systems at $60M), producing a median valuation of $225M with a range of $60M–$500M. The material spread reflects stage differential, with most peers at Series A.

Triangulating these two methods produces a low-to-high range of $10M–$259M, with a median of $118M. The wide range reflects genuine methodological tension: the stage-progression approach anchors conservatively to deployed capital, while the peer-median approach captures the substantial valuations achieved by defense-tech peers at more advanced stages. Key uncertainties include whether 9 Mothers' technology, traction, or market position warrant Series A comparables versus the Seed-stage baseline, and the relevance of public comp multiples given illiquidity and capital structure differences.
